nativeRange.habitat: Coastal to outer reef crests and slopes with rich invertebrate growth; generally found at depths of 0–60 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on coral tips (particularly Acropora), crustaceans, molluscs, sponges, tunicates, and algae in the wild. In captivity, tends to accept a varied meaty diet including squid, krill, clams, and hard-shelled shrimp. Hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 5692x3795. Tack-sharp full-body side profile of the classic half-yellow phase: grey body, yellow lower body/chin, scattered black spots, blunt dog-face snout and large eye. Best all-round representative.
H. Zell; 4920x3347. Sharp whole-body shot of the striking all-yellow phase with a few black spots on dark background; shows the species' color polymorphism.
Diego Delso; 5642x3761. Sharp in-situ full-body grey-brown phase densely covered in black spots with yellow pectoral fins; shows the classic spotted phase.

summary: The Arothron Dog Face Puffer is a personable Indo-Pacific reef fish with a rounded, blunt snout and large expressive eyes that give it a canine appearance. It can reach around 33 cm and is generally considered semi-aggressive, best kept singly in a large fish-only system where it tends to become notably interactive over time.
careNotes:
Arothron nigropunctatus is distributed throughout the Indo-Pacific, from East Africa to Micronesia and Samoa, north to southern Japan and south to New South Wales. In the wild it inhabits reef crests and slopes with rich invertebrate growth, at depths from the shallows down to about 60 m.

This species displays notable color polymorphism: individuals may appear grey-brown with scattered black spots, show a half-yellow phase with yellow on the lower body, or occasionally present an all-yellow phase. Color-phase transitions are rarely observed in captivity. The 'dog face' common name refers to the rounded, blunt snout and large expressive eyes that give the fish a canine appearance.

Like other Arothron puffers, this species has a beak-like structure formed by fused teeth that grows continuously throughout life. Offering hard-shelled foods such as clams and crab legs is generally advised to prevent dental overgrowth. It lacks pelvic fins and uses its pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins for locomotion.

Newly introduced specimens tend to be shy, but the species is commonly reported to become increasingly outgoing over time, and many keepers describe it as recognizing its keeper. This behavioral transition can take days to weeks.

The species is not reef-safe and will consume corals (particularly Acropora tips), crustaceans, molluscs, and sponges. It is best kept in a fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system — a setup that uses live rock for biological filtration and aesthetic structure without placing corals at risk — alongside robust tankmates of similar or greater size. Aquarists are advised to provide as much space as practical.

Like all puffers in the family Tetraodontidae, this species contains tetrodotoxin and is highly toxic to eat. Under significant stress, it may release skin toxins into the water; heavy filtration and a robust protein skimmer are generally advised. Transfer using a container rather than a net is recommended, as the species may inhale air while out of water — air ingestion can be fatal.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been documented in captivity. The species is oviparous in the wild. No captive breeding protocols are reported in the available literature.

additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Arothron nigropunctatus, Bloch & Schneider 1801), taxonomy (Tetraodontidae / Tetraodontiformes), maximum length (33 cm TL), native range (Indo-Pacific: East Africa to Micronesia and Samoa, north to southern Japan, south to New South Wales), temperature range (24.7–29.3°C from wild data), depth range (0–60 m), habitat (coastal to outer reef crest and slopes with rich invertebrate growth), and diet (corals, crustaceans, mollusks, sponges, tunicates, algae). Confirmed poisonous to eat (tetrodotoxin). IUCN Least Concern (2011).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (33 cm / 13 in), minimum tank size (1,500 L — significantly higher than LiveAquaria's 568 L hobby recommendation), salinity (~SG 1.026), temperament (mostly peaceful but threat to invertebrates), diet, and reef safety (not reef-safe). Wide tank-size disagreement (568 L vs 1,500 L, >2x) documented in careNotes; dataStatus set to needs_review per playbook step 3. Final tank value of 700 L is biased toward the cautious larger side from the nice-values list.

fish.breedingNotes: Not yet documented in captivity. Oviparous in the wild.
